chunk_summary: This section discusses ALT-based screening for pediatric steatotic liver disease in the context of obesity management, emphasizing metabolic risk, sex-specific ALT thresholds, and referral triggers for persistent elevation or other liver disease markers.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of ALT as a marker for systemic metabolic risk in pediatric obesity care.
Learn the sex-specific ALT thresholds for identifying abnormal liver function in children.
Recognize the workflow for managing elevated ALT, including lifestyle interventions and follow-up testing.
Identify referral triggers for pediatric gastroenterology or hepatology in cases of persistent ALT elevation or additional liver disease markers.
key_facts: ALT is a practical first-line test for pediatric steatotic liver disease screening in obesity care.
Sex-specific ALT thresholds: ~26 units/L for boys and ~22 units/L for girls (IAP guidelines).
Marked ALT elevation (>2x the local upper limit of normal) warrants evaluation for alternative etiologies.
Post-screening workflow includes repeating ALT/AST after 8–12 weeks of lifestyle changes targeting diet, activity, and sleep.
Referral to pediatric gastroenterology/hepatology is recommended for persistent ALT elevation or presence of markers like jaundice or hepatosplenomegaly.
reference_quote: The IAP obesity guideline uses sex-specific thresholds (around 26 units/L for boys and 22 units/L for girls) to flag abnormal values, and recommends evaluating for alternative etiologies when elevation is more marked (for example, >2 times the local upper limit of normal).
document_key_topics: ALT-based screening for pediatric steatotic liver disease
Sex-specific ALT thresholds and their clinical significance
Lifestyle interventions for managing elevated ALT
Referral criteria for pediatric liver disease specialists
